confirmedFindings.title: This session added currentArea to §6.4 GameState (and the DEV API) but the §6.8 persisted-surface list it rewrote in the SAME pass omits it
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.8 persist list lines 4186-4191; vs §6.4 line 4061 (currentArea) and §2.19 line 1621
confirmedFindings.evidence: The fix added to §6.4 (line 4061): `currentArea: AreaId; // where the player IS now (set by the `travel` intent) — non-derivable, persisted (§2.19 "current location")`. §2.19 (line 1621) lists "...equip state + durability, kill/clear/deed counts, current location, reputation/meters" as persisted. But the §6.8 enumerated persist surface — which the SAME session rewrote to add tier/live-combat/event-log/vitals (closing save-determinism-2) — reads (lines 4186-4189): "schemaVersion, RNG (seed + counter), clock, resources, producer counts, skill xp, base attributes, current vitals (hp / satiety / attribute-points), inventory, equipment, influence pillars (value + high-water + dents), stored tier, ranks, reputation, allegiance, flags, unlocked surfaces, quest status, counts, the live combat encounter ..., the capped event log, active-effect remainders, settings." — currentArea / current location is absent. So §6.4 + §2.19 say the player's area is non-derivable and persisted, but the authoritative §6.8 stored-surface list (rewritten this session) drops it. A literal §6.8 serializer would lose the player's location on save/load. This is a fresh inconsistency: the field was introduced and the list was expanded in the same diff, but they were not reconciled.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §6.8 is the authoritative "what gets serialized" enumeration the M0 save implementation builds against; an omission here means a save round-trip could silently reset the player to the start area despite §6.4/§2.19 promising it persists — and M0 (save spine) is explicitly the actionable build target now.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Insert "current area" into the §6.8 list immediately after "clock" (matching the §6.4 field ordering, where currentArea sits right after clock). Verbatim, unique, safe clerical edit.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed via git diff 0b60422..HEAD on docs/prd.md. Same pass added `currentArea: AreaId; // ... non-derivable, persisted (§2.19 "current location")` to §6.4 (line 4061) AND rewrote the §6.8 persist enumeration (lines 4186-4191) to add current vitals, stored tier, live combat encounter, and capped event log — but did NOT add "current area" to that list. §2.19 (line 1621) also lists "current location" as persisted. So §6.4 and §2.19 promise the player's area is non-derivable + persisted, yet the authoritative §6.8 serialization enumeration (which the M0 save spine builds against) omits it. A literal §6.8 serializer would drop player location on save/load. Fresh inconsistency introduced this session, not a rounds-1-3 restatement, not already fixed. Edit is purely clerical: insert "current area" after "clock" matching §6.4 field ordering; proposed_old string is unique (1 occurrence).
confirmedFindings.old_string: RNG (seed + counter), clock, resources, producer counts
confirmedFindings.new_string: RNG (seed + counter), clock, current area, resources, producer counts

confirmedFindings.title: M7 save-survival smoke tests never exercise the itch cross-origin EMBED, WebKit, or mobile-in-frame — and a same-session reload can't detect third-party partition/eviction; the single 28.5h autosave can silently vanish for real players while the gate stays green
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §7.2 M7 Definition-of-done (lines 4733-4736) + M7 phase 4 (line 4743) + phase 6 (line 4745); §7.3 (lines 4781-4785); §6.8 (lines 4176-4199)
confirmedFindings.evidence: M7 done-when (4733-4736): 'loading the live URL reaches the cold open and the first verb works; IndexedDB autosave + base64 export/import work against the deployed origin'. Phase 6 (4745): 'the live draft URL passes the fresh-browser smoke (... autosave persists across reload ...)'. Three structural problems for an itch.io HTML game: (1) itch serves HTML games inside a CROSS-ORIGIN iframe (html.itch.zone / html-classic.itch.zone), a different origin from the project page — that is the third-party context where Chrome/Firefox PARTITION IndexedDB and Safari/WebKit blocks-or-evicts it (ITP 7-day eviction). 'The deployed origin' / 'the live URL' opened directly is a TOP-LEVEL (first-party) context, so the smoke test as written specifically AVOIDS the partition that real players hit. (2) No engine is pinned: the only QA browser tooling (docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md lines 30, 207 — Playwright MCP + Chrome DevTools MCP) defaults to Chromium; the PRD names 'fresh-browser' but never requires WebKit, where the failure is worst. (3) 'autosave persists across reload' is a SAME-SESSION check — partitioned/ephemeral storage survives an immediate reload but is evicted hours/days later, so the criterion gives false durability confidence. No grep hit for 'safari', 'webkit', 'cross-origin', 'iframe', 'itch.zone', or 'partition' anywhere in docs/. §6.8 commits to a single autosave with no detection/fallback if the write silently no-ops.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the single highest-consequence deploy gap: the active-only / single-autosave model means there is exactly ONE copy of up to 28.5h of progress, living in precisely the storage class (third-party partitioned IndexedDB) that the deploy target treats as low-durability or blocks. The release gate can certify 'save works' and still ship a game where most players lose all progress, which is the most catastrophic outcome for a no-reset progression game whose entire pitch is the long climb.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Commit a v1 storage-durability POSTURE and convert it into M7 acceptance criteria. Add to M7 done-when: 'Autosave survives a reload tested INSIDE the actual itch embed (the iframe on the published/draft project page, not the raw asset URL), on BOTH a Chromium engine AND a WebKit/Safari engine, AND on a real mobile browser in the itch frame; AND the boot path detects unavailable/ephemeral IndexedDB (write-probe) and, on failure, surfaces a calm banner that directs the player to the base64 export backstop rather than silently no-op-ing the autosave.' Also set the itch page config explicitly (Mobile-friendly checkbox; fullscreen/viewport that preserves the §6.9 responsive reflow rather than letterboxing a fixed frame).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Substance verified and NEW. Grep confirms zero coverage of webkit/safari/cross-origin/iframe/itch.zone/partition/ITP/eviction/write-probe across docs/; QA tooling (docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md L29-30, 207) is Chromium-only; M7 done-when (prd.md 4734-4736) and the phase-4/6 smokes (4743, 4745) only assert same-session "autosave persists across reload" with no engine matrix and no storage-availability probe; §6.8 (4180,4198) locks a single autosave with no write-probe/fallback detection. itch.io's documented behavior of serving HTML games inside a cross-origin html.itch.zone iframe means real players hit third-party/partitioned IndexedDB (Chrome/Firefox partition, WebKit ITP eviction) that a same-session reload cannot detect — a real high-consequence gap for a no-reset 28.5h-progress game. Two caveats lower it from the claimed 'high': §6.8/§7.3 (4783) already designate base64 export as the explicit safety net against a cleared store (partial mitigation, just not proactively surfaced or auto-detected), and the phase-6 'live draft URL' smoke would in practice load the game inside the itch iframe (so the 'smoke avoids the partition' claim is overstated for that beat; the residual valid gaps are no-WebKit/Safari, no mobile-in-frame, no over-time-eviction detection, and no write-probe fallback). Per D-021 this is provisional §7 milestone detail → needs-human, not critical; does not block M0/M1.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">What storage-durability posture should v1 commit to for itch.io's cross-origin iframe embed, and should it become an M7 verify-gate acceptance criterion? Options: (a) accept ephemeral third-party storage and lean on a proactively-prompted base64 export as the real save (cheapest; PRD already names base64 as the safety net but never prompts it); (b) add a boot-time write-probe that detects unavailable/partitioned IndexedDB and surfaces a calm banner directing the player to export, plus require the M7 smoke to run inside the actual itch embed on BOTH a Chromium and a WebKit/Safari engine (and ideally a mobile-in-frame); (c) something stronger (e.g. redundant localStorage mirror). Trade-off: (b)/(c) are the only checks that actually catch silent autosave loss for most players, but add real QA scope (a WebKit engine + embed-context testing the current Chromium-only tooling does not cover) and a new core detection/fallback code path in §6.8.
confirmedFindings.question: What durability posture does v1 commit to for the itch cross-origin iframe: (a) accept ephemeral storage and lean hard on a proactively-prompted base64 export as the real save (cheapest), (b) detect-and-warn at boot when third-party IndexedDB is partitioned/blocked, or (c) something stronger? And do you accept adding 'test in the embed on Chromium AND WebKit AND mobile, with save-survives-reload-in-embed' as M7 verify-gate acceptance criteria (it is needed scope, but it is real added QA effort and the only place that catches this)?

confirmedFindings.title: The base64 export — the ONLY real backstop against silent storage loss on itch — is never surfaced on a revealed screen and the player is never prompted to back up
confirmedFindings.evidence: §7.3 (4783-4785) elevates export to load-bearing: 'Because itch.io is a static host with no server, the base64 export is the ONLY portable backup / hand-off path — the player's safety net against a cleared browser store.' But §2.19(b) (1613) only says 'the player CAN export a save to a text file and import it back' — it never says WHICH screen the export lives on, that the surface is revealed early, or that the player is ever PROMPTED/nudged to take a backup. There is no reveal-ladder entry that lights an export affordance, and the natural home (Settings) is not on the nav-reveal ladder at all (known finding accessibility-ux-1). So the documented safety net is, in practice, unreachable and undiscovered exactly when it matters (before a silent eviction).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: If finding deploy-host-matrix-1's storage risk is real, the export is the player's last line of defense — but a safety net the player never sees and is never reminded to use does not function. A first-time itch player who closes the tab and returns days later (Safari ITP window) finds nothing, with a backup mechanism they never knew existed. This is distinct from accessibility-ux-7 (import-overwrite confirm) and accessibility-ux-1 (a11y-controls entry point): the concern here is data-durability discoverability of EXPORT.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Spec that the export affordance is (a) reachable from a surface revealed by ~end of T0, and (b) proactively offered at low-cost moments (e.g. a gentle one-time 'back up your progress' nudge after the first tier-up, or on first long-session close), explicitly because itch's storage is non-durable. Add the export-reachability check to M7's smoke (export button findable from the cold-open-reachable shell).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Textual claims verified: §7.3 (lines 4782-4783) elevates the base64 export to "the only portable backup / hand-off path — the player's safety net against a cleared browser store," yet §2.19(b) (1613-1614) and §6.8 (4184) only say the player CAN export/import (portability/backup) with no UI placement and no prompt. The nav-reveal ladder §3.5 (1975-1983) reveals Skills/Combat/Map/House/Village/Region/Ties/Castle-town but NO Settings entry, while §6.9 (4219) confirms a Settings screen exists in the shell. So the documented safety net is never lit by the reveal ladder and is never proactively offered. The discoverability half overlaps accessibility-ux-1 (Settings missing from the reveal ladder), which the finding acknowledges as a dependency, but the proactive backup-nudge ask and the export-specific durability framing are genuinely new and not covered by accessibility-ux-1 or -7. It is a real coherence gap (§7.3's strong claim vs. §2.19/§3.5 silence). Severity is medium but contingent on deploy-host-matrix-1's eviction risk (IndexedDB is the durable primary; export is the backstop). It is a UX/design decision on the provisional layer that does not block M0/M1, hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: §7.3 calls the base64 export the player's ONLY safety net against a cleared browser store, but the PRD never says (a) on which revealed surface the export/import control lives or (b) whether the player is ever prompted to back up. Decision: (1) Which surface hosts export/import, and is that surface placed on the §3.5 nav-reveal ladder so it is reachable before any storage-loss event (note this is coupled to accessibility-ux-1, which already flags Settings missing from the ladder)? Options: add a Settings/About entry to the reveal ladder by ~end of T0, or surface export on an already-revealed early surface. (2) Do you want a proactive one-time "back up your progress" nudge, and if so when should it fire (e.g. after first tier-up, or on first long-session beforeunload) — or is silent IndexedDB durability plus a discoverable manual export sufficient? Trade-off: a nudge adds friction/noise to the deliberately quiet early UI but directly mitigates silent-eviction data loss on a static host. If adopted, add an export-reachability check to M7's smoke (export findable from the cold-open-reachable shell).

confirmedFindings.title: Audio is an unaccounted ASSET PIPELINE: risk-register R4 justifies 'low-risk art' by claiming 'no asset pipeline', yet §2.21/§6.9/M6 ship ambient beds + SFX with no licensing/credits surface, no bundle/load budget, and no autoplay-gesture handling — and M7 has zero audio acceptance criteria
confirmedFindings.evidence: R4 (4814) rests the 'Medium/Medium' art-risk rating on: 'The art register is locked low-risk (no asset pipeline: text + emoji + CSS, §6.9)'. But §2.21(a) (1674) commits 'Audio (canon): light ambient beds + UI/event SFX + mute toggle', M6 phase 7 (4720) ships 'a light ambient audio bed + UI/event SFX (all behind mute)', and §6.11 (4289) lists a mute toggle. Audio files ARE an asset pipeline: (1) licensing/credits/provenance — grep for 'credit/licens/attribut/provenance' finds NO credits or About-style surface; the only metadata surface is the commit-SHA stamp (M7 phase 2, line 4741), so CC-BY/royalty-free attribution required by most game-audio licenses has nowhere to live; (2) bundle size — audio beds add weight against the only load guard, 'load < 5 s to first interactable' (M7 phases 4/6), with no audio-size or lazy-load budget; (3) autoplay — browsers block audio before a user gesture and the cold open never specs gesture-gated start. M7's done-when (4733-4745) contains zero audio criteria.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The 'no asset pipeline' premise is the explicit basis for rating art/feel low-risk and for skipping process around it — but audio quietly reintroduces the exact pipeline (sourcing, licensing, credits, bundle weight, autoplay policy) that was claimed absent. Unlicensed/uncredited audio is a concrete legal/takedown exposure on a public itch page; oversized audio jeopardizes the <5s budget; un-gated autoplay throws console errors and may not play. None of it is gated by the release process.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either drop audio from v1 (making R4's 'no asset pipeline' literally true) or treat audio as a real, scoped pipeline: add an M7 audio checklist — (a) every clip's license is permissive + recorded in an in-game Credits/About surface (reuse the Settings/About surface from M7 phase 2); (b) an audio bundle-size/lazy-load budget so the <5s load holds; (c) audio starts only after the first user gesture (the 'Open your eyes' click) and respects mute/reducedMotion. Correct R4 to acknowledge audio as the one asset that is not text+emoji+CSS.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. R4 (line 4814) literally justifies the low-risk art rating with "no asset pipeline: text + emoji + CSS, §6.9", but audio is committed canon in §2.21(a) (line 1674), §6.9 (lines 4226-4228), and M6 phase 7 (line 4720). Repo-wide grep confirms zero coverage of audio licensing/credits/attribution/provenance, autoplay/user-gesture handling, or any audio bundle/lazy-load budget; the only metadata surface is the M7 commit-SHA stamp (line 4741) and the only load guard is "<5s to first interactable" (M7 phases 4/6). M7 done-when (lines 4733-4745) has no audio acceptance criteria. The "no asset pipeline" framing is consistently visual-scoped (even the subset font is rationalized as "not an art asset"), and audio — the one element outside text+emoji+CSS+SVG — is never reconciled with it; the implementation (sourced files vs synthesized) is unspecified, which is the gap itself. Genuine, new internal inconsistency plus a deploy-milestone coverage hole. Not refutable. Per D-021, a provisional §7-process item that does not block M0/M1, so medium/needs-human rather than critical; the fix requires a scope decision (drop audio to make R4 literally true, vs scope a real audio process: permissive licensing + in-game Credits surface + bundle/autoplay/mute budget + M7 audio criteria), so not trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.question: Audio (§2.21(a)/§6.9/M6) is the only v1 element outside the "text + emoji + CSS" register, yet R4 rates art-risk low on the premise of "no asset pipeline" and M7 ships with zero audio acceptance criteria — no licensing/credits surface (only the commit-SHA stamp exists), no bundle/lazy-load budget against the <5s load guard, and no autoplay-gesture gating (browsers block pre-gesture audio). Which path do you want: (A) park audio from v1 so R4's "no asset pipeline / low-risk" posture is literally true; or (B) treat audio as a real scoped pipeline and add an M7 audio checklist — (a) every clip permissively licensed + attributed in an in-game Credits/About surface (reuse the M7 phase-2 Settings/About surface), (b) an audio bundle-size/lazy-load budget that keeps <5s load, (c) audio starts only after the first user gesture (the "Open your eyes" click) and respects mute + prefers-reduced-motion? Also: should audio be specified as synthesized (Web Audio, no files → no licensing/bundle exposure) or sourced clips? Either way, correct R4 to acknowledge audio as the one asset outside text+emoji+CSS.

confirmedFindings.title: satietyMax has no formula anywhere — M1's satiety bar has no defined cap
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.3 (L954) + §2.7 (L1079) + §4 (no formula); needed by §7.2 M1 phase 3 (L4521)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.1 (L2781) defines hpMax = `40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp`, but NO satietyMax / satiety-capacity formula exists anywhere in §4. §2.3 Vitals is `{ hp, hpMax, satiety, satietyMax, fatigue }` '(derived caps recomputed on load from attributes + gear + milestones)' and §2.7 (L1079) says 'Character level + XP grants HP, satiety capacity, attribute points' — but neither gives a base value or formula. M1 phase 3 (L4521) builds the satiety bar ('soft stamina/satiety drains on labour and soft-throttles the action rate ... with a rest verb recovering it'), which requires a satietyMax to bound, display, and compute the drain/refill against.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The soft-stamina/satiety meter is a core M1 deliverable and its DoD asserts 'stamina throttles-not-blocks'. Without satietyMax you cannot render the bar, compute the fraction that feeds the throttle, or write the M1 test. It also transitively depends on the undefined characterLevel, so the gap is two-deep.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a satietyMax formula to §4 parallel to hpMax (e.g. `SATIETY_BASE + perLevel·characterLevel + attrTerm`), or commit a fixed M1 stub constant (e.g. 100) and defer the scaling. Either way M1 needs a committed value.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: `satietyMax` appears only in the Vitals struct (§2.3 L954) as a "derived cap recomputed from attributes + gear + milestones," and §2.7 (L1079) promises "satiety capacity" scales with level — yet no satietyMax formula exists anywhere in §4. By contrast §4.6.1 (L2781) gives an explicit hpMax formula in the same Vitals family. §3.8 (L2204) only defers the "soft-stamina RATE," never the capacity/cap, so the max is neither specified nor explicitly deferred — a genuine asymmetric gap, not a rounds-1-3 restatement nor reconciled. M1 phase 3 (L4521) builds the satiety bar and the renderer (L4524) paints it, both of which need a bound to compute the throttle fraction. Not critical because any provisional cap trivially unblocks (consistent with §4's provisional-numbers philosophy), but the choice between a scaling formula (parallel to hpMax) vs a flat M1 stub is a design decision, hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: satietyMax has no formula while its sibling hpMax does (§4.6.1: `40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp`). §2.7 says "satiety capacity" grows with character level. Pick one for M1: (A) commit a scaling formula in §4 parallel to hpMax, e.g. `satietyMax = SATIETY_BASE + perLevel·characterLevel + attrTerm` (attrTerm likely VIT/STR) — keeps the leveling reward in §2.7 real but adds a new constant to tune at M6; or (B) commit a fixed M1 stub (e.g. `satietyMax = 100`) and defer the level/attribute scaling to a later milestone — fastest to build/test now, but temporarily makes §2.7's "satiety capacity grows" promise inert. Which, and if (A), what base/per-level/attribute coefficients?

confirmedFindings.title: 'vitals' vs 'character' field-name drift — M0 builds GameState against a field §6.4 doesn't have
confirmedFindings.evidence: M0's stored surface (phase 2, L4490) lists 'vitals'; §6.8 (L4187) lists 'current vitals (hp / satiety / attribute-points)'; §2.3 (L954) defines a type `Vitals { hp, hpMax, satiety, satietyMax, fatigue }`. But the canonical §6.4 GameState (L4057-4082) has NO `vitals` field — hp/satiety/attributePoints live in `character: { hp: number; satiety: number; attributePoints: number; attributes: Record<AttrId, number> }` (L4065-4066). The very first thing M0 builds is GameState, against an inconsistently-named field.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Minor, but it is the first type the build touches; the milestone's surface list should reference the real §6.4 field so the M0 coder doesn't invent a `vitals` slice that has to be reconciled later. (NEW: no existing type-data-model finding covers this.)
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Point M0's surface list at the canonical §6.4 `character` field (and optionally unify §6.8/§2.3 wording later). Conservative clerical edit applied to the M0 line only.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: §6.4 GameState (L4057-4082) has no top-level `vitals` field — hp/satiety/attributePoints are stored under `character` (L4065-4066). The M0 phase-2 surface list (L4490) lists `vitals` alongside otherwise-real §6.4 field names (schemaVersion, rng, clock, resources, flags, unlocked, log), so `vitals` is the only entry that doesn't match the canonical struct. §6.8 (L4187, "current vitals (hp/satiety/attribute-points)") and §2.3 (L954, `Vitals { hp, hpMax, ... }`) use "vitals" as a human-facing concept layer, which is why the M0 list drifted. Real, low-severity clerical inconsistency on the very first type M0 builds. Fix is a unique, judgement-free clerical edit pointing the M0 line at the real §6.4 `character` field; §6.8/§2.3 conceptual wording left untouched.
confirmedFindings.old_string: (M0 surface: schemaVersion, rng, clock, vitals, resources, flags, unlocked, log)
confirmedFindings.new_string: (M0 surface: schemaVersion, rng, clock, character {hp, satiety, attributePoints} — the §6.4 stored field; there is no separate top-level `vitals`, resources, flags, unlocked, log)

confirmedFindings.title: Forward-only migration chain has no guard for a FUTURE-version save (downgrade) opened by an older build
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.8: "`schemaVersion` is stored; on load, an ordered list of `migrate_vN_to_vN+1(save)` steps runs in sequence to bring an old save current". §6.13 #3 (line 4326): "ordered forward migrations indefinitely + a pre-migration raw backup". The chain is forward-ONLY. If `save.schemaVersion > the running build's version` (a player on a stale cached tab/PWA, or who re-imports a base64 backup made on a newer build), there is NO forward step to run — the chain is a no-op and the save is left at a version the code does not understand. §6.8 never states what happens; the "degrade gracefully" bullet (4195) only names a "corrupt/unreadable save", not a future-version one. The build would happily run selectors over a structure it cannot interpret.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: itch.io aggressively caches; a player who once loaded v5 and later hits a CDN-stale v4 tab, or who re-imports a backup, silently runs an old build against a newer save → undefined reads, mis-rendered or zeroed pillars, possibly a clobbering autosave that downgrades their real progress. The locked 'never wipe Influence/holdings' invariant can be violated by a silent downgrade.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a version-ceiling clause: a save whose schemaVersion exceeds the build's is treated as incompatible and routed through the same calm degrade-gracefully recovery as a corrupt save, with the raw bytes preserved for re-import in a newer build — never a silent half-load or downgrade-overwrite.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed against docs/prd.md §6.8 (lines 4192-4197) and §6.13 #3 (lines 4326-4329). The migration chain is explicitly forward-only (`migrate_vN_to_vN+1`), and the degrade-gracefully clause (line 4195) covers only a "corrupt/unreadable save" — a future-version save is structurally valid, so the forward chain is a no-op and the older build runs selectors over a structure it cannot interpret. Grep across docs/ and brainstorms/ found no existing future-version/downgrade/version-ceiling handling (all "ceiling" hits are the unrelated narrative banzuke ceiling), so this is genuinely NEW and not reconciled. The scenario (itch.io/PWA stale cache, or re-importing a newer base64 backup into an older build) is plausible and the silent-downgrade harm can violate the LOCKED "never wipe Influence/holdings" invariant. Fix is the obvious safe-default hardening with no contested trade-off — extend the existing recovery path to a version ceiling — so trivial-apply. Target string at line 4194 is unique.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">function, unit-tested). Migrations live in `persistence/migrations/`.
confirmedFindings.new_string:
function, unit-tested). Migrations live in `persistence/migrations/`. **A save whose `schemaVersion` exceeds the running build's** (a future-version save opened by an older / CDN-stale build — there is no forward step to run) is treated as **incompatible** and takes the same calm degrade-gracefully recovery as a corrupt save, with the raw bytes preserved for re-import in a newer build — never a silent half-load and never a downgrade that overwrites the real autosave.</verified_new>
</invoke>

confirmedFindings.title: No game-identity tag and no save↔registry referential-integrity check — a foreign or edited import can pass and corrupt the run
confirmedFindings.evidence: The §6.4 GameState envelope opens with `schemaVersion` and `rng` but has NO app-identity/magic field. So a base64 blob from a *different* game (or any unrelated text that happens to base64-decode to a JSON object with a plausible `schemaVersion`) can pass a naive validate. Separately, the save stores keyed references — `producers: Record<ProducerId,…>`, `inventory: Record<ItemId,…>`, `quests: Record<QuestId,…>`, `equipment[...].equipDefId`, `unlocked: Set<SurfaceId>` — but nothing validates those ids against the live content registries at import time (the §6.6 verifier runs at TEST time only). A hand-edited save, or one from an older content set, can reference an `ItemId`/`QuestId`/`ProducerId` that no longer exists; selectors then read undefined and break silently mid-run.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Both are 'fail-safe vs. corrupt-the-run' decisions. Without a magic/appId, a foreign import isn't rejected; without registry-integrity, a stale/edited id is loaded and detonates later. Choosing reject-vs-drop-unknown-ids is a genuine policy call, and adding an identity field touches the locked GameState envelope.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a constant game-identity/magic field to the save envelope that import must match, and add an import-time referential-integrity pass over all stored ids against the live registries; the human picks reject-whole-save vs. drop/default-unknown-ids-and-warn.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Partly confirmed, narrowed. The §6.4 envelope (prd.md:4057-4082) indeed has no app-identity/magic field and stores keyed refs (producers/inventory/quests/equipDefId/unlocked); the §6.6 verifier (4132) runs at build/test time, not import; §6.8 (4185/4195) only commits to validate + graceful recovery for a "corrupt/unreadable" save. The magic-field half is weak/speculative — a normal structural validate already rejects most foreign blobs, and absence of a magic constant does not by itself let a run be corrupted. The real kernel is the registry-integrity half: a structurally-valid save with a now-removed ItemId/QuestId/ProducerId parses fine, so it never trips the corrupt-save recovery path and can read undefined later. But this is substantially mitigated already: the ordered migration chain (4192-4194) is the designed path for content/shape changes shipped with a deploy, and it is a single-player, single-deploy, single-autosave game so save and reload share an identical content set; the only un-mitigated vectors are hand-edited/foreign imports and unversioned content edits. What is genuinely unspecified is the policy fork for a structurally-valid-but-stale id (reject-whole-save vs drop/default-and-warn). Low severity given the existing graceful-degradation + migration discipline; needs-human per D-021 (a §6 provisional/tech policy call that does not block M0/M1).</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">For the base64 import / load path (§6.8), two robustness policies are currently unspecified beyond "validate + degrade gracefully": (1) Should the save envelope carry a constant game-identity tag (e.g. `app: "kami-kakushi"`) that import must match before attempting migration, to fast-reject foreign/unrelated blobs that happen to base64-decode to a schemaVersion-bearing object? (2) After migration, should load run a referential-integrity pass over all stored ids (inventory ItemIds, quests QuestIds, producers ProducerIds, equipment equipDefIds, unlocked SurfaceIds) against the live content registries, and on a stale/unknown id choose (a) reject the whole save into the existing calm-recovery flow — safest for integrity, or (b) drop/default the unknown ids and continue with a logged warning — preserves more progress across content changes but risks silently losing items/quests? Note this only bites on hand-edited/foreign imports and unversioned content edits, since normal content changes ship with a unit-tested migration; the choice is mainly about how defensively the import path treats inputs the migration chain did not author.
confirmedFindings.question: Should the save envelope carry a constant game-identity/magic field (e.g. `app: "kami-kakushi"`) that import must match — and should import validate every stored id against the live registries, choosing (a) reject the whole save to corrupt-recovery, or (b) drop/default unknown ids and continue with a warning? (a) is safest for integrity; (b) preserves more progress across content changes but risks silently losing items/quests.

confirmedFindings.title: Raw pre-migration backup lifecycle is undefined — atomicity of the multi-step chain, where the backup lives, when it's deleted, and how restore is triggered are all unspecified
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.8: "A pre-migration backup of the raw bytes is kept so a failed migration is recoverable." This single sentence leaves every operational question open: (1) Atomicity — when `migrate_v2_to_v3` throws after `migrate_v1_to_v2` succeeded, is the chain run purely in-memory with one final atomic commit (so the stored autosave is untouched), or does each step write back (leaving a half-migrated record)? (2) Where is the backup stored — a separate IndexedDB key, so a later autosave can't clobber it? (3) When is the backup retained vs. deleted (only on failure? always? overwritten next migration?)? (4) Is restore automatic (on detecting a thrown migration) or manual? (5) Is there a post-chain assertion that the final schemaVersion === build version, to catch a *missing* intermediate migration that silently leaves the save under-migrated?
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the rollback story the task specifically asks for. 'A backup is kept' is not a recovery mechanism. If the backup shares the autosave slot, the next debounced autosave (which fires 'on a tick interval', §6.8) destroys it; if the chain writes per-step, a mid-chain throw corrupts the stored save the backup was meant to protect.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify: run the whole migration chain in-memory and commit once atomically; store the raw pre-migration bytes under a separate, never-autosaved IndexedDB key; define retention + restore-trigger; and assert final schemaVersion === build version after the chain.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: §6.8 (lines 4196-4197) states only "A pre-migration backup of the raw bytes is kept so a failed migration is recoverable" with no operational lifecycle. The supporting docs (ADR D-013a line 138; risk R3 line 4813; locked-decisions line 289) repeat the same one-liner policy ("ordered forward migrations + raw backup") but none specify: (1) chain atomicity (in-memory single commit vs. per-step write-back), (2) where the backup lives relative to the explicitly "single autosave slot" at line 4180 — a real concern because the debounced autosave (lines 4182-4183) could clobber a backup sharing that slot, (3) retention/deletion, (4) restore trigger. Notably the recovery UI (4195-4196) offers "re-import or a fresh start" and never references restoring from the kept backup, so the backup's role in recovery is disconnected from the stated recovery path. (5) No post-chain schemaVersion-equals-build assertion to catch an under-migrated save. This is a genuine, specific gap, distinct from the migration-policy items settled in earlier rounds. It is a bounded tech-spec design decision (not an M0 blocker), so medium/needs-human per D-021 framing.
confirmedFindings.question: Define the migration/backup lifecycle for §6.8. Pick a policy for each: (a) Commit model — run the whole ordered migration chain in-memory and write back ONCE atomically on success (stored autosave untouched on a mid-chain throw), or write back per step? (b) Backup location — store the raw pre-migration bytes under a SEPARATE IndexedDB key that the debounced autosave never touches (recommended, since §6.8 mandates a single autosave slot that would otherwise clobber it), or reuse the autosave slot? (c) Retention — keep the backup until the next clean load / N successful saves, or delete immediately on a successful chain? (d) Restore trigger — auto-rollback to the backup and surface a recovery UI on a thrown migration, or expose the backup only as a manual export? Note the current recovery UI (re-import / fresh start) never references the backup. (e) Add a post-chain assertion that final schemaVersion === build version to catch a missing intermediate migration?

confirmedFindings.title: Save-WRITE failures (quota-exceeded, transaction abort, torn mid-autosave) have no handling — graceful-degrade only covers READ
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.8 lines 4180-4183 (Primary store: IndexedDB / autosave cadence)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.8: "Autosave fires on a debounced cadence (after meaningful intents and on a tick interval) and on `visibilitychange`/`beforeunload`." Every robustness sentence in §6.8/§2.19 is about LOAD ('corrupt/unreadable save shows a calm recovery'). There is zero spec for a failed WRITE: IndexedDB `put` can reject with QuotaExceededError (store full), the DB can be unavailable in private/Safari modes, or a `beforeunload`-triggered transaction can abort before commit (a torn write). Nothing says the autosave must be a single atomic `put` in ONE transaction (vs. clear-then-write, which leaves an EMPTY slot if interrupted), and nothing surfaces a write failure to the player. (Distinct from the round-3 completeness-critic-1 iframe-AVAILABILITY finding: here the DB opens fine but the write itself fails or tears.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Silent autosave failure is worse than a load error — the player believes they're saved, keeps playing, then loses everything on reload. On a backend-free static host the base64 export is the only safety net, so the player must be told the moment a write fails so they can export.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify autosave as a single atomic `put` within one transaction (never clear-then-write), and surface a calm persistent 'couldn't save — export a backup' notice on a write rejection; human confirms loudness and whether to auto-prompt the base64 export on first failure.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed real and new. §6.8 (docs/prd.md lines 4180-4183) specifies autosave cadence on debounced intents and visibilitychange/beforeunload, but neither §6.8 nor §2.19 specifies any WRITE-failure behavior. All robustness language is LOAD-side: "A corrupt/unreadable save shows a calm, explained recovery" (4195-4197) and "degrades gracefully" (§2.19, 1615-1616). A repo-wide grep for quota/atomic/torn/transaction-abort/write-fail finds nothing covering a rejected IndexedDB put, no atomicity guarantee (single put vs clear-then-write, the latter leaving an empty slot if interrupted), and no surfacing of a write failure to the player. Distinct from the round-3 iframe/availability finding (DB unavailable to open) — here the DB opens but the write fails or tears mid-commit. Silent autosave failure is a genuine data-loss risk on a backend-free host where the base64 export is the only safety net. Resolution requires UX judgment (notice loudness, whether to auto-prompt export) so it is needs-human; per D-021 a provisional §6 tech-spec gap that does not block M0 is not critical, hence medium.
confirmedFindings.question: How should a FAILED autosave write be handled (the spec currently covers only failed reads)? Trade-offs: (A) Specify autosave as a single atomic `put` in one transaction (never clear-then-write, which can leave an empty slot if interrupted at beforeunload) AND surface a calm, persistent "couldn't save — export a backup now" notice on any write rejection (QuotaExceededError / transaction abort / private-mode unavailability) — consistent with the existing "calm recovery, never a scary wall" ethos, at the cost of one more UI state and a write-result code path. (B) Atomic put + silent retry only, no player-facing notice — simpler, but risks silent data loss (player keeps playing believing they're saved). (C) Atomic put + on FIRST write failure auto-prompt the base64 export flow — loudest/safest but most intrusive. Decision needed: confirm the atomicity guarantee (option A's single-transaction put), the notice loudness, and whether the first failure should auto-prompt the base64 export.

confirmedFindings.title: Japanese-script runs are never lang-tagged — kanji/macron terms read as Unicode names or garbage to a screen reader (WCAG 3.1.2)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.11 lists only 'Semantic structure + live region ... screens/panels use semantic landmarks and labelled controls' (line 4287-4288) and 'full keyboard operability' (4282) — nothing about marking up the language of the heavy Japanese-script content. The UI is saturated with kanji that carry primary meaning: pillar labels '武威 / 官威 / 家産 / 家格 / 家威' (ui-design line 137, 344-350), season tags '春 夏 秋 冬' (line 137), and macron romanizations 'gōshi ... jitō-dai' (line 175-176). A page whose root is lang="en" with no lang="ja" on the kanji spans makes an English-voice screen reader either spell the glyphs, read their Unicode point names, or skip them silently; the macron Latin words want lang="ja-Latn". This is WCAG 3.1.2 (Language of Parts) and is ORTHOGONAL to round-2 accessibility-ux-5 (text alternatives): even a correct aria-label in Japanese must be lang-tagged to pronounce. grep confirms zero 'lang=' occurrences in either doc.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The pillar names, season, and rank labels ARE the information architecture of a text-only UI. Unpronounceable/garbled labels make the four-pillar panel and the rank ladder unusable to a screen-reader user, defeating the 'text-first, legible on any device' canon.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a §6.11 requirement that every Japanese-script run is tagged lang="ja" (and romanized terms lang="ja-Latn"), and that the macron/CJK lint (§6.6) is extended to flag untagged CJK in rendered strings.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source. §6.11 (prd.md:4287-4288) lists only ARIA live region, semantic landmarks, and labelled controls — nothing about marking the language of the heavy Japanese-script content. grep across docs/ returns zero `lang=` and zero `3.1.2`/`Language of Parts`/`ja-Latn`. The kanji content that carries primary meaning is real and load-bearing: pillar labels 武威/官威/家産/家格/家威 and season tags 春夏秋冬 (ui-design.md:137), plus macron romanizations gōshi/jitō-dai (ui-design.md:175-176). The doc commits to a text-first UI where these labels ARE the information architecture, so untagged kanji under an English-root page degrades screen-reader output (WCAG 3.1.2, Level AA). This is genuinely NEW and orthogonal to round-2 accessibility-ux-5 (text alternatives): a correct Japanese aria-label still needs lang-tagging to pronounce. The §6.6 macron/romanization lint exists (prd.md:4142) so the suggested lint extension is coherent. Fix is a clean additive bullet to the existing a11y-basics list, squarely within already-locked canon §H a11y-basics intent — no design trade-off, so trivial-apply. Medium severity (spec gap in §6 tech, not blocking M0/M1, but real AA compliance gap for the core UI).
confirmedFindings.old_string:
- **Semantic structure + live region:** the event/story log is an ARIA live region so reveals/important events
  are announced to assistive tech; screens/panels use semantic landmarks and labelled controls.
confirmedFindings.new_string:
- **Semantic structure + live region:** the event/story log is an ARIA live region so reveals/important events
  are announced to assistive tech; screens/panels use semantic landmarks and labelled controls.
- **Language of parts (WCAG 3.1.2):** every Japanese-script run (kanji pillar/rank/season labels) is marked
  `lang="ja"` and romanized terms `lang="ja-Latn"`, so assistive tech pronounces them instead of reading
  Unicode glyph names or skipping them; the §6.6 romanization lint also flags any untagged CJK in rendered strings.

confirmedFindings.title: `--ink-faint` text role fails WCAG AA contrast on all three paper surfaces — and it carries the locked-hint / disabled-reason / caption text
confirmedFindings.evidence: Computed sRGB contrast of `--ink-faint #7A6C59` against the paper surfaces: on `--washi #F3E9D2` = 4.23:1, on `--washi-shade #E7D9BC` = 3.66:1, on `--washi-deep #DDCFAE` = 3.31:1 — ALL below the WCAG AA 4.5:1 floor for normal text (and the micro pips at `--fs-micro 0.75rem`, line 166, are well under any 'large text' exemption). The palette assigns `--ink-faint` to exactly the text a low-vision player most needs to read: 'muted labels, locked hints, captions' (line 46), the next-rung 'silhouette with its requirement' (§5.4 line 361), and the disabled-button 'reason on tap/focus' rendered '`--ink-faint`' (§5.5 line 369). Secondary: `--beni` negative-delta text drops to 4.42:1 / 4.00:1 on shade/deep, and an `--ai-soft` bar FILL vs a `--washi-deep` track is only 2.44:1 (< the 3:1 non-text-contrast needed to see how full a meter is).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Unlock conditions and 'why is this disabled' reasons are the load-bearing affordance text of a 'UI as progression' game; if they fail contrast, the player who most needs the hint cannot read it. The palette is LOCKED, so this is a deliberate human call, not a clerical fix.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified independently: --ink-faint #7A6C59 (docs/ui-design.md line 46) computes to 4.23:1 on --washi #F3E9D2, 3.66:1 on --washi-shade #E7D9BC, and 3.31:1 on --washi-deep #DDCFAE — all below the WCAG AA 4.5:1 normal-text floor. The doc assigns this exact token to functional, low-vision-critical text: "muted labels, locked hints, captions" (line 46), the next-rung "silhouette with its requirement" (§5.4 line 361), and the disabled-button "reason on tap/focus" (§5.5 line 369). The micro pips at --fs-micro 0.75rem = 12px (line 163-166) are normal-size text, so no large-text exemption applies. Secondary claims also reproduce exactly: --beni #A93B47 negative-delta text = 4.42:1 (shade) / 4.00:1 (deep); --ai-soft #5C8B93 bar fill vs --washi-deep track = 2.44:1 (< the 3:1 non-text floor for a meter). No contrast/WCAG spec exists anywhere in ui-design.md or PRD §2.21/§6.11 to reconcile or waive this; ink-faint is used for genuinely functional text (also the line-321 system log tier), not purely decorative. The palette is locked design intent under D-018, so a fix is a human design/taste tradeoff, not clerical — needs-human is correct (not blocking M0/M1, so not critical per D-021 framing). Genuinely new: rounds 1-3 left no contrast note in any of these files.
confirmedFindings.question: The --ink-faint #7A6C59 text role fails WCAG AA (4.5:1) on all three paper surfaces (4.23 / 3.66 / 3.31), yet ui-design.md assigns it to load-bearing affordance text — locked-rung unlock hints (§5.4), the disabled-button "why" reason (§5.5), and captions/system-log lines. Since the D-018 palette is locked intent, which path do you take? (a) Darken --ink-faint toward ~#6A5A45 (≈4.5:1 on --washi, still <AA on the darker surfaces — so also restrict locked-hint/disabled-reason text to the --washi base surface); (b) keep --ink-faint as a decorative/muted-label token only and render all locked-hint + disabled-reason text in --ink-soft #4A3F33 (8.49:1, passes everywhere); or (c) accept a documented sub-AA tradeoff for muted captions only, with an explicit note in §2 that hint/reason text must not use it. Same call needed for --beni text on shade/deep cards (~4.0:1) and the --ai-soft-fill / --washi-deep-track meter (2.44:1, below the 3:1 non-text floor — consider --ai #27496D fill or a darker track).

confirmedFindings.title: The keyboard-only / screen-reader acceptance test covers only the cold-open + one rung — the dense late UI (combat panel, four-bar Influence, map, modals, tooltips) is never operability-verified
confirmedFindings.evidence: The only a11y operability gate is M7 task 6: '... all live and verified by a keyboard-only and a touch-only run of the cold open + one rung' (line 4719; line 4708 repeats 'cold open + a rung'). The cold open is one verb + the log — the trivial single-column state. The hard keyboard/SR surfaces all appear LATER and are never in scope: the combat panel's stance/ability/item/retreat verbs (ui-design §10 R3), the bar-by-bar four-pillar Influence panel (§5.3), the Map screen (§10 R6), focus-trapped Escape-closable modals (§5.10), and focus/tap tooltips (§5.9). This is DISTINCT from round-2 accessibility-ux-6 (textScale layout reflow): this is keyboard/SR *operability* scope.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Modal focus traps, tab navigation across ~9 revealed nav entries, and the combat verb set are exactly where keyboard/SR operability breaks — and the acceptance test deliberately never reaches them, so the release gate would pass with the complex UI unverified.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Extend the M7 acceptance run to drive (via __qa to a late state) the combat panel, the four-bar Influence panel, a modal, and the map under keyboard-only and screen-reader.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed at docs/prd.md line 4719 (M6 task 6) and echoed at line 4708 (M6 DoD): the keyboard-only/touch-only operability run is scoped to "the cold open + one rung." §6.11 (lines 4282-4283) mandates "full keyboard operability" as a system requirement, but the verification gate never reaches the dense revealed UI — combat verbs, four-bar Influence panel, modals, map. Those surfaces appear only in task 8's capture sweep (line 4721) as screenshots, not as keyboard/SR operability runs, so the hardest a11y surfaces (focus traps, tab order across revealed nav, combat verb set) can rot undetected even though the task is explicitly "wired so they cannot rot." Distinct from a textScale/reflow finding; this is operability scope. Fix is a low-judgement alignment that reuses the already-listed signature states.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">verified by a keyboard-only and a touch-only run of the cold open + one rung.
confirmedFindings.old_string: verified by a keyboard-only and a touch-only run of the cold open + one rung.
confirmedFindings.new_string: verified by a keyboard-only and a touch-only run of the cold open + one rung AND of a late state (force-loaded via `window.__qa`) exercising the combat panel (stance/ability/item/retreat), the four-bar Influence panel, a focus-trapped modal, and the map screen — so operability is proven on the dense revealed UI, not only the single-column open.

confirmedFindings.title: No skip-link and no named landmarks — keyboard/SR users tab through the header vitals + the growing (~9-entry) nav rail to reach the hero log on every screen
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.11 promises generic 'semantic landmarks' but names none (no banner/nav/main, no accessible name on the log region) and there is no skip-to-content / skip-to-log mechanism. The desktop DOM order is header (vitals) → nav rail → workspace (ui-design §4.1), and the nav grows to ~nine ordered entries by T2 ('single column → Skills → Combat → Map → House → Village → Region → Ties → Castle-town stub', §5.6 line 376). So a keyboard or screen-reader user must traverse the vitals and the entire nav before reaching the event log — 'the heart of the screen' — on every screen change. This is distinct from round-2 accessibility-ux-1 (Settings entry point) and ux-3 (mobile tab-bar count).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Repeatedly tabbing past a long, ever-growing nav to reach the primary content is a textbook keyboard/SR friction failure on a single-page app whose whole value is the log.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Require named ARIA landmarks (banner / nav / main, plus an accessible-named live-region log) and a skip-to-log/skip-to-content link.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source: §6.11 (prd.md:4287-4288) commits only to generic "semantic landmarks" and an ARIA live-region log — it names no banner/nav/main landmarks, gives the log no accessible name, and a grep of docs/ finds no skip-to/skip-link anywhere. §6.9 (4218-4221) plus ui-design §4.1 (198-203) and §5.6 (374-376) confirm the header-vitals → growing nav rail → workspace/log order, with the rail reaching ~9 ordered entries by T2. So keyboard/SR users do traverse vitals + the whole nav before the hero log on each screen, and the skip-link/named-landmark remedy is a genuine gap distinct from round-2's Settings-entry and mobile-tab-count items. It is a minor refinement of an already-stated §6.11 commitment — standard a11y practice with no design trade-off — hence low/trivial-apply. (Placed in §6.11's existing landmarks bullet, the natural home, rather than the §6.9 nav bullet the finding proposed.)
confirmedFindings.old_string:
- **Semantic structure + live region:** the event/story log is an ARIA live region so reveals/important events
  are announced to assistive tech; screens/panels use semantic landmarks and labelled controls.
confirmedFindings.new_string:
- **Semantic structure + live region:** the event/story log is an ARIA live region (with an accessible name)
  so reveals/important events are announced to assistive tech; the shell uses **named ARIA landmarks**
  (`banner` header, `navigation` rail, `main` workspace) and labelled controls, plus a **skip-to-log /
  skip-to-content link** so keyboard and screen-reader users jump straight to the content instead of tabbing
  through the growing (~9-entry) nav on every screen.

confirmedFindings.title: LogMessage's 4-value `channel` enum omits the milestone/reveal/rank-up tier — the one line class that MUST be assertively announced has no channel to route politeness on
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.1c types the log line as `LogMessage { id, text, channel ('narration'|'reward'|'combat'|'system'), colorClass, tICK }` — FOUR channels. But ui-design §5.1's log table defines FIVE tiers, adding 'milestone / reveal / rank-up' as a distinct, highest-leverage class (line 322). That fifth tier — exactly the line that must be announced assertively for 'reveal = plot' — has no `channel` value, so the renderer cannot map per-channel ARIA politeness (routine narration polite, milestone/reveal assertive) the way round-2 accessibility-ux-4's flooding fix requires. (The same line also carries the `tICK` typo for `tick`.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Per-channel politeness is the realistic fix for live-region flooding; if the milestone class is not a typed channel, the most important announcement can't be promoted to assertive without ad-hoc renderer logic, undermining the announce-reveals-not-noise goal.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add 'milestone' to the channel enum so politeness can be routed by channel.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified verbatim: docs/prd.md:898 types LogMessage.channel as a 4-value enum ('narration'|'reward'|'combat'|'system'), while docs/ui-design.md:316-322 is a table whose first column is literally headed "Channel" and lists FIVE rows, the fifth being 'milestone / reveal / rank-up' (line 322, called the highest-leverage juice surface). ui-design itself names these "channels," so the PRD enum (4) and the ui-design channel table (5) are inconsistent, and the omitted tier is the very line that should be announced most prominently. The auditor's stronger justification (per-channel ARIA polite/assertive routing required by round-2) is overstated — no politeness mapping is documented anywhere (docs only say the log is an ARIA live region) and colorClass could carry visual treatment — but the documentation inconsistency the suggested fix targets is real, new, and unfixed. Adding 'milestone' aligns the enum with ui-design's table. The same line also carries the verifiable tICK->tick typo, fixed in the same edit.

confirmedFindings.title: No confirm/undo on irreversible in-game actions, and the single continuously-overwriting autosave removes the universal 'reload last save' recovery — a cognitive-accessibility risk
confirmedFindings.evidence: The save model is a single autosave that 'restores the exact saved GameState' with no time-skip (§6.8 line 4198) and no manual slots. The only confirm modals named are 'a save/load confirm, a quest-accept' (ui-design §5.10 line 406) — there is no required confirmation or undo for irreversible in-game actions (consuming materials in crafting, sell, or the consequential branching narrative-route commitments such as the T3 peaceful-vs-assertive choices). With one autosave continuously overwriting, the 'reload my last save' escape hatch that mitigates misclicks in most games does not exist. This is DISTINCT from round-2 accessibility-ux-7 (import/fresh-start overwriting the slot): this is in-game destructive/irreversible verbs + the loss of the reload fallback. WCAG 2.2 3.3.4/3.3.6 (Error Prevention) applies.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Players with cognitive/motor differences are most harmed by an irreversible misclick that cannot be reloaded away; on a single autosave there is no recovery path, so an unguarded destructive verb is permanent.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Cited text verified verbatim: §6.8 specifies a single autosave that "restores the exact saved GameState" with "no time-skip catch-up" (lines 4180, 4198), and ui-design §5.10 names confirm modals only for save/load and quest-accept (line 406). §6.11 a11y "basics" lists scalable text, colourblind cues, keyboard/touch, reduced motion, pause, live region, mute — but no confirm/undo for destructive verbs. The PRD in fact LOCKS irreversible commitments: §4.4 line 2679 "NO RESPEC IN v1 — allocations are committed... permanent" and line 2684 "you can't undo a choice." With a single overwriting autosave and no manual reload slot, an irreversible misclick (material-consuming craft, sell, committed allocation) has no recovery path — a recognized WCAG 2.2 3.3.4/3.3.6 error-prevention concern. The "never a wipe / dents not wipes" philosophy (lines 416-418, 756-757) softens economic stalls but does not cover an in-the-moment destructive misclick, so it does not fully reconcile the finding. Distinct from round-2 accessibility-ux-7 (import overwriting the slot). Low severity and a genuine design decision (which action classes warrant a confirm/undo), hence needs-human per D-021.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Which classes of irreversible in-game action — material-consuming craft, sell, committed attribute-point allocation (§4.4 no-respec is LOCKED), and later T3 narrative-route commitments — should require a confirm step or a brief undo window, given the single continuously-overwriting autosave (§6.8) provides no "reload last save" recovery? Options: (a) confirm only on high-cost/rare actions (e.g. consuming a rare material, selling equipped/last-of-kind, allocating an attribute point) and leave routine fast-loop verbs unguarded; (b) a short undo window on the last destructive intent instead of a modal (less friction in the incremental loop); (c) status quo, relying on the "dents not wipes" philosophy and accepting WCAG 3.3.4/3.3.6 non-conformance for game actions. Trade-off: confirms add friction to a fast incremental loop, but allocation and narrative-route choices are genuinely unrecoverable and cognitively/motor-impaired players are most harmed by an unrecoverable misclick. Record the chosen action-class policy in §2.19/§6.11 (and note it against §6.8's single-autosave model).
confirmedFindings.question: Should irreversible/destructive in-game actions (material-consuming craft, sell, consequential narrative-route commitments) require a confirm step (or a brief undo window), given the single continuously-overwriting autosave provides no 'reload last save' recovery? Trade-off: confirms add friction to a fast incremental loop, and the 'no reset / dents-not-wipes' philosophy already softens economic mistakes — but narrative-route choices and material consumption are genuinely unrecoverable. Decide which action classes get a confirm/undo and record it in §2.19/§6.11.

confirmedFindings.title: Repo/game has no LICENSE file and no declared license, yet ships PWYW on itch from a public-style repo (rights ambiguous)
confirmedFindings.location: README.md (whole file, 24 lines); CLAUDE.md; repo root (find -iname 'LICENSE*' returns nothing); docs/prd.md §7.3 line 4795
confirmedFindings.evidence: `find . -iname "LICENSE*"` returns no file; README.md contains no license section, and `grep -ni 'licen[sc]e|open[- ]source|all rights|copyright'` across README.md/CLAUDE.md/docs returns zero license declarations. Meanwhile §7.3 line 4795 plans to '...set pricing to free / pay-what-you-want' on itch.io, and the README publicly links the project as an inspiration-derived build. With no LICENSE, the default is 'all rights reserved' — which silently forbids any reuse/forking and is invisible to itch downloaders.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The single most basic legal artifact for any released project is missing. Whether the game's CODE and CONTENT (prose, balance data) are open-source, source-available, or all-rights-reserved is an unmade, human-only decision; it affects what the PWYW community may do with it, whether the repo can be public, and what notice the itch page must carry. It is a one-line clerical fix once decided, but the decision itself is the human's.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide the game's own license (e.g. MIT/Apache-2.0 for code + CC-BY/CC0 or proprietary for content/prose, or all-rights-reserved) and add a top-level LICENSE (and, if split, a CONTENT-LICENSE) plus a one-line license note in README; reference it from the itch page copy.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed by source: `find . -iname "LICENSE*"` returns nothing; `grep -niE 'licen[sc]e|open[- ]source|all rights|copyright'` across README.md/CLAUDE.md/docs/prd.md returns zero hits; README.md (24 lines) has no license section and publicly links two inspiration GitHub repos; docs/prd.md §7.3 line 4795 plans 'free / pay-what-you-want' on itch.io. No license/legal mention exists anywhere in docs/, brainstorms/, human-in-the-loop/, or memory/, and decisions.md (through D-021) records no license decision — so this is genuinely new, not a rounds-1-3 restatement, and not already fixed. Real gap, but downgraded from claimed 'medium' to LOW: it is non-blocking (a license is only required at the M7 deploy gate, far from the current M0/M1 frontier per D-021) and the legal default (all-rights-reserved) is safe if ambiguous. It is a human-only decision (choice of code vs content license, public-repo status), not a clerical edit, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: Before any public release / itch.io upload (M7), decide the project's license(s). (a) CODE: permissive (MIT / Apache-2.0) vs source-available vs all-rights-reserved? (b) NARRATIVE/CONTENT & balance data: CC-BY / CC0 vs proprietary? (c) Is the repo intended to be public? Trade-off: a permissive code license fits the 'built agentically, inspired by two OSS games' ethos and invites reuse/learning/contribution, but lets others reskin the Edo-Japan world; all-rights-reserved protects the world but is unusual for a PWYW hobby release and blocks community forking. Once decided, the fix is clerical: add top-level LICENSE (and a separate CONTENT-LICENSE if split) + a one-line note in README + reference it from the itch page copy.

confirmedFindings.title: Display fonts: license never specified and OFL distribution obligations (bundle OFL.txt + retain copyright) are absent from the build/deploy plan
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §3.1 lines 116-119 and §3.2 lines 142-144; docs/prd.md §7.3 line 4776 (build:itch)
confirmedFindings.evidence: ui-design.md §3.1 names Shippori Mincho / Shippori Mincho B1 / Yuji Syuku / Zen Kaku Gothic New / Noto Sans JP (and §574 open-choice alternates Zen Old Mincho / Klee One / Hina Mincho). §3.2 line 142-144 recommends 'Option B (best ... fully offline, recommended): self-host a hand-subset built once with `pyftsubset`/`glyphhanger`'. confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The legal lens question — 'is the font LICENSE specified and redistribution-safe for a web build?' — is answerable YES on the merits (OFL) but the PRD never records it, and the recommended self-hosting path redistributes the font binary inside dist/. Shipping a self-hosted OFL .woff2 WITHOUT the accompanying OFL.txt + copyright header is a license violation. The deploy checklist must carry a 'bundle font license' step or the very first itch zip is non-compliant.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Record each chosen font's license (OFL) in ui-design.md §3.1 and add to §7.3 build:itch / M7 a step: 'copy the OFL license text + each font's copyright notice into dist/ (e.g. /fonts/LICENSE-OFL.txt) so the redistributed subset carries its license.'
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified on the merits and confirmed NEW. ui-design.md §3.1 (L116-119) names Shippori Mincho / Shippori Mincho B1 / Yuji Syuku / Zen Kaku Gothic New / Noto Sans JP; §3.2 (L142-144) recommends the self-hosted-subset path ('Option B ... fully offline, recommended: self-host a hand-subset built once with pyftsubset/glyphhanger') and L149 ships /fonts/shippori-subset.woff2. All named families are in fact Google Fonts under the SIL OFL, which obligates the license text + copyright notice to travel with any redistributed/self-hosted copy; the recommended Option B redistributes the .woff2 binary inside dist/ with no accompanying OFL.txt — a (minor, common) license breach on a public PWYW release. NOT a rounds-1-3 restatement: the prior battery review (brainstorms/2026-06-26-prd-battery-review.md:1216) audited AUDIO licensing, IndexedDB partitioning, emoji, and zip layout, but never font/OFL distribution. Set needs-human (not trivial-apply): the font picks are explicitly DRAFT/flagged-for-human (ui-design.md L112), so the license cannot be finalized clerically, and adding a deploy step is an execution-plan judgement; per D-021 the provisional §7/M7 deploy detail is needs-human, not critical (M7-only, does not block M0/M1).
confirmedFindings.question: The DRAFT font stack (ui-design.md §3.1: Shippori Mincho, Shippori Mincho B1, Yuji Syuku, Zen Kaku Gothic New, Noto Sans JP — final pick flagged for you) is entirely SIL Open Font License (OFL) Google Fonts. The recommended Option B (self-host a pyftsubset/glyphhanger .woff2 in /fonts/) redistributes the font binary inside dist/, and the OFL requires the license text + each font's copyright notice to ship alongside it. Currently no doc records any font license, and build:itch (prd.md §7.3 L4776) has no license-bundling step. Decide: (1) Confirm the final font picks are all OFL (current candidates are). (2) Approve adding to ui-design.md §3.1 a one-line license note ('all families OFL') and to build:itch / M7 a deploy step: copy each font's OFL text + copyright header into dist/ (e.g. /fonts/LICENSE-OFL.txt) so the redistributed subset carries its license. Trade-off: Option B is offline/private but obligates shipping the OFL file; Option A (Google dynamic subsetting) sidesteps redistribution entirely but adds a network dependency that contradicts the offline/no-network smoke-test (prd.md L4743) and the privacy stance. Which path, and approve the OFL-bundling step for the first itch zip?

confirmedFindings.title: Subsetting an OFL font but keeping its Reserved Font Name ('Shippori Mincho') likely breaches OFL clause 3
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.2 declares `@font-face { font-family: "Shippori Mincho"; src: url("/fonts/shippori-subset.woff2") ... }` — i.e. a hand-subset (a Modified Version under the OFL) served under the original family name. The SIL OFL's Reserved Font Name clause (§3) prohibits a Modified Version from using the original's Reserved Font Name(s) without written permission; the OFL FAQ treats subsetting as a modification and advises renaming when redistributing the modified binary.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the subtle, real OFL trap for self-hosted Japanese display fonts (the lens explicitly flags that many JP fonts are not freely embeddable — here they are OFL, but the RFN rule still bites). A subsetted file distributed under the Reserved Font Name is technically out of compliance even though the font is 'free'. Easy to get right, easy to get wrong.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either (a) rename the subset's internal font-family (e.g. 'Kurosawa Mincho Subset') and reference that name in CSS, or (b) use Option A (Google's served CSS, no local modification, RFN preserved by Google) — and note the RFN rule in ui-design.md §3.2.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed real and new. Shippori Mincho ships under OFL 1.1 with a Reserved Font Name (verified), and SIL's official webfont/RFN guidance treats glyph-subsetting as a Modified Version that "would not normally allow the use of Reserved Font Names." ui-design.md §3.2 (lines 142-152) recommends Option B: self-host a hand-subset built with pyftsubset/glyphhanger, served as font-family "Shippori Mincho". pyftsubset preserves the original name table (RFN intact) unless explicitly renamed, and the doc gives no rename guidance or any license/attribution note (grep across all docs found zero OFL/RFN/license mentions) — so a naive build of the recommended path ships a Modified Version still carrying the RFN, a genuine compliance trap. Caveat noted: the CSS font-family value is a local alias, not the binary name table, so the @font-face line alone doesn't itself breach OFL; the exposure lives in the distributed subset file. Severity is low, not the claimed medium: the font is deferred to M1 and not locked, the magnitude is a single name-table string, and the same section already offers a fully-compliant alternative (Option A, Google-served, RFN preserved by Google). needs-human because the fix is a design choice (rename the self-hosted subset vs adopt Option A), not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: For the kanji-subset font (ui-design.md §3.2, currently deferred to M1), how do you want to stay OFL-compliant given Shippori Mincho's Reserved Font Name? Option 1: keep the recommended self-hosted subset (Option B) but rename its internal name table during the pyftsubset build (e.g. "Kurosawa Mincho Subset") and reference that name in CSS — preserves offline self-hosting and the no-asset-pipeline purity, costs one build-flag and a CSS family rename. Option 2: switch to Option A (Google-served CSS) — Google preserves the RFN, zero local modification, but adds a runtime network dependency. Either way, add a one-line RFN/OFL-attribution note to §3.2 so the build step doesn't silently ship a Modified Version under the reserved name.

confirmedFindings.title: Font delivery 'Option A' (Google dynamic subsetting) contradicts the LOCKED 'zero network calls' deploy criterion and adds a GDPR exposure
confirmedFindings.location: docs/ui-design.md §3.2 line 140 and open-choice #2 lines 593-594; docs/prd.md §7.3 lines 4743 and 4745
confirmedFindings.evidence: ui-design.md §3.2 line 140 lists 'Option A (zero build step): Google's dynamic subsetting — link the CSS and the browser downloads only the unicode-range slices' and §574 open-choice #2 still presents Option A as a live pick for the human. But §7.3 line 4743 makes 'zero network calls' a smoke-test acceptance criterion ('...AND there are zero network calls and no `__qa` surface present') and line 4745 repeats 'no backend / no network calls'. Option A fetches CSS + woff2 from fonts.googleapis.com / fonts.gstatic.com at runtime, which (a) fails the zero-network smoke test and (b) transmits each player's IP to Google — the basis of the German Google-Fonts GDPR ruling for EU-reachable sites.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Two governing docs disagree on a release gate, and the dispreferred option also carries a privacy/GDPR cost for an EU-reachable itch release. Leaving Option A 'open' invites a build that silently fails the human-blessed zero-network acceptance test.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Close Option A: state in ui-design.md §3.2 / open-choice #2 that Google dynamic subsetting is INCOMPATIBLE with the §7.3 zero-network-calls criterion and raises a GDPR IP-disclosure concern, and lock self-hosted Option B (already the recommendation).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim: ui-design.md §3.2 line 140 lists "Option A (zero build step): Google's dynamic subsetting" and open-choice #2 (lines 593-594) still presents Option A as a live pick (recommending B). prd.md §7.3 line 4743 makes "zero network calls" a smoke-test acceptance criterion and line 4735 / 4745 repeat "no backend, no network calls"; the static/offline guarantee is a LOCKED hard constraint (D-013/canon §H, prd.md line 3907 "no server, no backend"). Option A fetches CSS+woff2 from Google at runtime, which genuinely contradicts that locked criterion and adds an EU/GDPR IP-disclosure exposure. Finding is NEW (no prior-round font/network decision in decisions.md or brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md) and not reconciled (Option A is recommended-against but never marked incompatible). Severity capped at low rather than medium because Option B is already the standing recommendation in both locations, the §7.3 smoke test would catch a wrong build, font delivery is deferred to M1, and the item blocks neither M0 nor M1 (per D-021 framing this is a needs-human open-choice closure, not a critical defect).
confirmedFindings.question: Font delivery is still flagged as an open choice for the human (ui-design.md open-choice #2), but the two options conflict with a LOCKED constraint. Option A (Google dynamic subsetting, zero build step) fetches CSS + woff2 from fonts.googleapis.com / fonts.gstatic.com at runtime, which violates the locked static/offline guarantee (D-013, canon §H) and would fail the §7.3 "zero network calls" smoke-test criterion, and transmits each player's IP to Google (the basis of the German Google-Fonts GDPR ruling for EU-reachable sites). Option B (self-hosted hand-subset .woff2 via pyftsubset/glyphhanger) is fully offline and already the recommendation, at the cost of a one-time subset build step. Should Option A be eliminated outright — i.e. mark it INCOMPATIBLE with the zero-network criterion in §3.2 and open-choice #2 and lock self-hosted Option B as mandatory — accepting the one-time subset build step?

confirmedFindings.title: No content rating / content warnings planned for itch despite child-spiriting, drowning, grief, debt and violence themes
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §7.3 lines 4794-4795 (itch page setup) and §7.2 M7 step 5 line 4744; §1 premise (e.g. §1.3/§1.7 the weir where he was 'found', §1.8/§5 the grieving family)
confirmedFindings.evidence: The itch page-setup steps (§7.3 lines 4794-4795 and M7 step 5 line 4744) cover only 'Kind = HTML', 'played in the browser', viewport, and 'free / pay-what-you-want' — there is NO content-rating / content-warning / age-descriptor step. A `grep -ni 'content rating|content warning|age rating|mature|adult content'` across docs returns nothing. Yet the premise is literally kamikakushi (神隠し — a child 'spirited away'/abducted), the protagonist was 'found' at a weir (drowning/death imagery), a family grieves a son believed dead, debt/poverty drive the plot, and there is on-screen combat/killing.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: itch.io exposes content-rating and content-warning settings (and an 'adult content' toggle) that the uploader must set; child-disappearance, drowning and grief are exactly the themes itch's content-settings UI exists to flag. Shipping with these unset is both a compliance gap and a discoverability/expectations risk for players. It is a human-owned classification call, missing from the deploy runbook the agent is supposed to author.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a deploy-checklist step in §7.3 / M7: set itch content rating + content warnings/descriptors appropriate to the themes (violence, death/drowning, child-abduction folklore, distressing themes), and surface a brief content note in the page copy.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified: §7.3 deploy brief (docs/prd.md lines 4792-4795) and M7 step 5 (line 4744) enumerate itch settings (Kind=HTML, browser-played, viewport, free/PWYW) but include NO content-rating/warning/descriptor step; grep across docs/README/brainstorms returns no content-rating/warning/age-descriptor/adult-content terms. The themes are genuinely present in §1 (kamikakushi child-disappearance §1.3, the weir, the grieving family §1.8, debt-driven plot, on-screen combat §2.8). Genuinely new — prior-round brainstorm hits are unrelated (estate-stage names, Sayo/Sato collision). Severity downgraded medium→low: itch.io does not mandate content ratings for mild folklore/violence themes (its adult-content gate targets explicit content), and §7.3 is explicitly a 'brief' with the full upload runbook deferred to M7 step 5 — a provisional §7 milestone deliverable per D-021, not a blocker for M0/M1. It is a legitimate completeness gap and a human classification call, hence needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The itch.io deploy runbook (§7.3 / M7 step 5) sets Kind/viewport/pricing but never declares a content rating or content warnings, despite themes of child-disappearance (kamikakushi), drowning/death imagery, grief, debt, and on-screen combat. Before the human-gated publish, what classification should the page declare? Options: (A) leave unrated — itch does not require a rating for non-explicit folklore/violence and the themes are mild, accepting minor expectations-mismatch risk; (B) add a deploy-checklist step to set a content warning/descriptor (e.g. 'mild fantasy violence; themes of death, child-disappearance and grief') plus a one-line content note in the page copy, leaving the adult-content toggle OFF; (C) (B) plus an explicit age guidance line. Trade-off: (B/C) improve player expectation-setting and discoverability at near-zero cost; (A) is lowest-effort but omits the classification a uploader is prompted for. This is your classification call and only affects the deferred M7 runbook, not M0/M1.</parameter>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: What content rating and content-warning descriptors should the itch page declare (e.g. 'Mild violence / themes of death, child-disappearance, and grief')? This is your classification call and must be set before publish.

confirmedFindings.title: No in-game Credits / Attribution surface is specified; the half-referenced 'Settings/About' surface is undefined and absent from the nav-reveal ladder
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.9 lines 4218-4219 (nav list) vs §7.2 M7 step 2 line 4741
confirmedFindings.evidence: The nav list at §6.9 lines 4218-4219 enumerates 'Estate / Village / Wilds / Skills / Combat / Influence / Map / Journal / Settings' — no Credits/About/Attribution surface. confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Once fonts (OFL) and any later assets are bundled, and given the README cites two inspiration projects, the product needs a place to carry credits/attribution and authorship — both a genre convention and, for any CC-BY assets, a hard requirement. confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md: the §6.9 nav list (lines 4218-4219) enumerates Estate/Village/Wilds/Skills/Combat/Influence/Map/Journal/Settings with no Credits/About/Attribution surface, and the sole mention of a 'Settings/About surface' is M7 step 2 (line 4741), scoped only to a build commit-SHA stamp.
